questions.text: I hold the view that consistency matters more than intensity in achieving goals.
I am comfortable exercising authority even when it creates tension.
I often push for my preferred approach in group decisions.
I find that my emotional steadiness during others' hardships allows me to remain present and constructive long after emotional engagement would have exhausted me.
I design my workflows to minimize friction and maximize repeatability.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the reality that tests whether my ethical commitments are genuinely held or merely professed.
I notice that my emotional baseline is remarkably stable from day to day.
I have been told that I feel things more intensely than most people.
I find that I instinctively know which topics are safe and which are landmines in a given group.
I rarely think about what someone left unsaid after a conversation.
I am attuned to the difference between genuine interest and polite listening.
I find that focusing on what I want—rather than what others expect—leads to my best life.
When resources are scarce, I instinctively direct them toward other people first.
I tend to go without rest to make sure the people around me are supported.
I find that my emotional responses ramp up faster than I can consciously process what's happening.
I believe that the best creative work happens when all structure is removed.
I believe that taking someone's suffering seriously, even when it is complex or inconvenient, is a form of moral courage.
I find that I am usually the one helping the group uphold its own standards.
